entry.title: Bitcoin covenants are inevitable
entry.summary:
Eric Voskuil presents a thought-provoking look at the sustainability of Bitcoin's economic model by considering transaction fees as a potential sole support for mining activity in the absence of significant block rewards. He posits that if blocks average 800kB, achieving 0.4 BTC per block through fees alone—equating to roughly 50 satoshis per virtual byte (sat/vb)—could be feasible. This results in a transaction fee of $2 for a 200-byte transaction when Bitcoin is valued at $20,000, escalating to $100 at a Bitcoin price of $1 million.
Voskuil further delves into Bitcoin’s future, forecasting the finance of mining operations through transaction fees versus block rewards. He notes that currently, a block reward of approximately 1.7% per annum of 19 million BTC supports the existing level of mining activity. If we were to reduce the block reward to 0.1% per annum of 21 million BTC, a Bitcoin value of around $310,000 would be required to maintain today's mining effort. According to the Bitcoin halving schedule, this level of block subsidy will continue until 2036, when it decreases to about 0.0986% annually, potentially extendable by four years assuming over 1.5% of Bitcoin's supply is permanently lost. This analysis offers a fascinating glimpse into the long-term viability of Bitcoin’s economic framework and its dependency on market dynamics and technological advancements.
